abstract: Replacing energy-intensive cryogenic distillation with highly efficient adsorptive separation remains a primary goal in chemical engineering, yet achieving both high selectivity and high capacity through molecular sieving-type adsorbents is a persistent challenge. Here, we report the development of **UTSA-400**, an oxyfluoride-based ultramicroporous metal-organic framework with the formula [Ni(WO₂F₄)(pyz)₂] (pyz = pyrazine). This material features one-dimensional pore channels engineered to accommodate propylene molecules with optimal binding affinity while strictly excluding propane. Within the framework, exposed oxide/fluoride pairs serve as potent functional sites that strengthen host-guest interactions, resulting in significantly enhanced propylene uptake. In contrast, propane molecules are entirely rejected due to the precisely regulated dynamics of the host framework. This robust binding environment allows propylene to reach near-saturation levels within the pore confinement under ambient conditions, maximizing pore space utilization and achieving superior packing density. The nature of these enhanced interactions was elucidated through a combination of in situ infrared spectroscopy and dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Breakthrough experiments confirm that **UTSA-400** can directly produce polymer-grade (>99.5%) propylene with remarkable dynamic productivity. This work demonstrates how atomic-precision pore engineering can optimize guest binding to overcome the traditional trade-offs in adsorptive gas separation.
